dialogue.utterance: This 911 call is now complete. Care transfer and patient report are fully handed to the ED staff.
Skin has normal color and no evidence in reports of rashes, wounds, or bruising.
Pupils are equal and reactive to light.
No oral bleeding or obstruction found.
No chest deformities observed and breath sounds are tight with wheezing.
Abdomen is soft and findings noted per documentation.
All extremities are mobile with intact pulses bilaterally.
The patient is alert but mildly disoriented to time.
No visual signs of dehydration found in notes.
Patient is now alert, though not fully oriented, consistent with field notes.
His state of breathlessness remains the primary concern despite the slight decline in tachycardia.
As best as I can tell, out of the blue, he had a sudden exacerbation consistent with past medical reports.
No visual loss or drooping of the eyelids observed.
He's not showing signs of swallowing difficulties or neck pain.
No drainage from nose, ears, or mouth found.
No voiding difficulty was reported by either the patient or the wife.
No pelvic instability or tenderness found.
Skin turgor is normal, showing no signs of dehydration consistent with documentation.
No indication of bug bites or stings in incident summary.
We'll need to reassess your responsiveness following the initial treatments to monitor any change.
Your respiration rate is 23, consistent with earlier findings.
EKG remains stable but continues to reflect tachycardia as noted in outcome.
No medications were brought to the field by the patient or his wife.
Let's reassess after the nebulizer treatment to see if breathing improves.
Oxygen saturation remains stable, though we will keep a close eye on it.
Continuous monitoring for any changes in ETCO2 levels or overall respiratory status as per protocol.
Chippenham is the selected facility for definitive care as per the family statement earlier.
Keeping the patient upright during transport is best for effective ventilation.
Applying three belts and a harness per safety protocol.
Transport crew will remain on alert throughout so movement is kept smooth and secure.
Hospital is aware of our arrival time by an earlier radio transmission.
Preparations are in place for a smooth transfer as soon as we reach the ED.
All medical devices are intact and ready for use by the hospital staff.
We are bringing you a patient with acute COPD exacerbation and no response to albuterol alone.
The patient is remaining normotensive with stable pulse during the ride to the hospital.
No issues expected during the handover as all procedures and protocols have been completed as documented.
No additional drugs administered at transfer point. All medication details are in the field record.
If you have questions about meds or past history, please contact the 911 care team directly as the wife may have more.
Paper folder is with him to ease hospital interpretation and expedite any needed medical actions.
All pre-protocol applied successfully and any documentation will be handed to shift medic as per our in-house SOPs.
We've maintained continuous patency and deliver O2 via nasal cannula at 6 LPM fully operational without compromise.
Expected hospital ED arrival in 13 minutes with transfers to be almost simultaneous with arrival.
Vital signs checked 5 minutes ago. Pulse 102, Resp 23, BP 136/80, Glucose 295, SpO2 98.
A left arm IV was chosen to ensure optimal access and easy to read for monitoring needs.
Paperwork awaits at ED-17. I'll give it to the nurse once we arrive and complete the physical handover.
Straps arranged do not restrict movement or circulation and current distress level seems minimal.
We are maintaining a semi-upright as that is known best for his breathing and overall maintenance.
He's a known COPD patient who recently had an exacerbation with wheezing and DIB but otherwise is still coherent.
Wrote note in folder: "Known COPD with high glucose. Albuterol and Ipratropium used early in treatment."
Hospital ED team will process for first response to ED and decide on immediate interventions.
If any labs are drawn or O2 adjusted, note them in patient chart like we requested at the start.
Mild disorientation to time but otherwise oriented.
Obvious self-talk is minimal, cooperative, and about to ramp into smoother hospital-based intervention.
Observing his condition closely with time.
All action items closed out.
This is sign off per EMT best practices as all data points are valid for hospital doctors to act upon.
Chippenham Hospital, Unit 3 en route with a patient for ED-17. Timed-in at 20 minutes transport delay.
We have a 74-year-old male COPD with exacerbation with difficulty breathing and DIB on arrival. All information and treatment patches are ready for a safe handoff.
This 911 call is now complete. Care transfer and patient report are fully handed to the ED staff.
